draft.introduction: Whatever one remembers at the moment of death determines one’s next state of being. Krishna urges Arjuna to remember him at the hour of death and promises union.

translation.text: And whoever, at the time of death, leaves the body remembering me alone, he attains my state of being; of this there is no doubt. Whatever state of being one remembers when one gives up the body at the end, to that state one goes, being always absorbed in that thought.
translation.original_text: anta-kāle ca mām eva smaran muktvā kalevaram | yaḥ prayāti sa mad-bhāvaṃ yāti nāsty atra saṃśayaḥ

translator_notes.note: The doctrine of death-thought has parallels in Tibetan Buddhism’s bardo teachings and in the Christian ars moriendi tradition. The underlying principle is that consciousness at death is maximally concentrated and therefore maximally determinative.
